title: Baloch Militants Kill 10 at Chagai Copper Mine; Af-Pak Non-Escalation Holds Day 14; Pakistan Deports 146,000+ Afghans
detail:
On April 23, 2026, approximately 40 armed Baloch militants on motorcycles and vehicles stormed the National Resources Private Limited (NRL) copper and gold mining facility in the Darigwan area of Chagai district, Balochistan province. The attack, which occurred around 5:45 PM local time, killed 10 people: 7 workers — including at least one Turkish national — and 3 security personnel. Attackers set fire to drilling equipment, generators, and heavy machinery; explosions in fuel tanks caused multiple fatalities. Pakistan's Frontier Corps and security forces cordoned the area and launched a pursuit operation; no group immediately claimed responsibility, though the attack bore the hallmarks of the Baloch Liberation Army (BLA), which launched coordinated attacks across Balochistan in February 2026 killing over 190 people. Chagai district, site of Pakistan's 1998 nuclear tests at Ras Koh and Kharan, hosts significant strategic infrastructure; the NRL facility is part of a broader push by Pakistani authorities to exploit copper reserves estimated at tens of billions of dollars. The attack reflects the ongoing security vacuum created by the concentration of Pakistan's military and intelligence bandwidth on the Af-Pak border conflict and US-Iran mediation — leaving internal security pressures unaddressed.

On the Afghanistan-Pakistan bilateral front, April 23 marked the 14th consecutive day without major cross-border military incidents under the Urumqi non-escalation framework — extending the longest unbroken lull since Operation Ghazab-il-Haq commenced on February 26, 2026. Both Taliban and Pakistani officials maintained their commitment to a second round of China-mediated talks, with Urumqi Round 2 expected in mid-to-late May 2026. The non-escalation framework continued to hold despite the absence of a formal ceasefire or written agreement — sustained by mutual exhaustion, Chinese economic leverage, and diplomatic momentum from Pakistan's successful US-Iran ceasefire extension brokering on April 22.

Human Rights Watch published a major report on April 21 — generating continued policy responses on April 23 — documenting a surge in Pakistan's forced deportation of Afghan refugees, with over 146,000 Afghans expelled from Pakistan in 2026 alone. HRW documented arbitrary detentions, inadequate or no deportation notices, and returns of particularly vulnerable individuals including former Afghan government employees, journalists (at least 9 confirmed forced returns), human rights defenders, and activists who face persecution or death under Taliban rule. Pakistan's mass deportation campaign has accelerated since April 1, 2026, coinciding with the non-escalation framework — suggesting Pakistan is leveraging refugee expulsion as a pressure tool against Kabul even while military operations pause. International organizations including Refugees International called on Pakistan to immediately halt the deportations pending proper asylum screening, citing violations of the principle of non-refoulement under international refugee law. The Taliban government condemned the deportations but took no retaliatory action, consistent with the non-escalation posture.

media.caption: 10 killed in Baloch militant attack on copper/gold mine in Chagai district, Balochistan; Af-Pak non-escalation framework holds Day 14
HRW documents Pakistan surge in forced returns of 146,000+ Afghan refugees in 2026, including journalists and activists facing Taliban persecution
